q: If $x$ is the number of electrons of $C a(20)$ with $n+l+m=3 . \mathrm{y}$ is the number of radial nodes in orbital $4 d_{z^2} . \mathrm{w}$ is the number of angular nodes in $4 d_{z^2}$ orbital and q is the number of nodal planes in $3 p_z$ orbital then, find the value of $[x+y+w+q]$ ?
question: If $x$ is the number of electrons of $C a(20)$ with $n+l+m=3 . \mathrm{y}$ is the number of radial nodes in orbital $4 d_{z^2} . \mathrm{w}$ is the number of angular nodes in $4 d_{z^2}$ orbital and q is the number of nodal planes in $3 p_z$ orbital then, find the value of $[x+y+w+q]$ ?
solution:
The atomic number of calcium ($Ca$) is $20$. Its electronic configuration can be written as:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^2$$

To find $x$, the number of electrons with $n + l + m = 3$, we will check the quantum numbers for each occupied subshell. A fully occupied orbital holds $2$ electrons.

For the $1s$ subshell: $n=1, l=0, m=0$

$$n+l+m = 1 + 0 + 0 = 1$$

For the $2s$ subshell: $n=2, l=0, m=0$

$$n+l+m = 2 + 0 + 0 = 2$$

For the $2p$ subshell: $n=2, l=1$, and $m$ takes values $-1, 0, +1$

  • If $m = -1$, $n+l+m = 2 + 1 - 1 = 2$

  • If $m = 0$, $n+l+m = 2 + 1 + 0 = 3$ (This orbital holds $2$ electrons)

  • If $m = +1$, $n+l+m = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4$

For the $3s$ subshell: $n=3, l=0, m=0$

$$n+l+m = 3 + 0 + 0 = 3$$ (This orbital holds $2$ electrons)

For the $3p$ subshell: $n=3, l=1$, and $m$ takes values $-1, 0, +1$

  • If $m = -1$, $n+l+m = 3 + 1 - 1 = 3$ (This orbital holds $2$ electrons)

  • If $m = 0$, $n+l+m = 3 + 1 + 0 = 4$

  • If $m = +1$, $n+l+m = 3 + 1 + 1 = 5$

For the $4s$ subshell: $n=4, l=0, m=0$

$$n+l+m = 4 + 0 + 0 = 4$$

Adding the electrons that satisfy the condition gives $2 + 2 + 2 = 6$. So, the value of $x$ is $6$.

Next, we look for $y$, which is the number of radial nodes in the $4d_{z^2}$ orbital. The formula for the number of radial nodes is $n - l - 1$.

For a $4d$ orbital, the principal quantum number $n = 4$ and the azimuthal quantum number $l = 2$.

$$\text{Radial nodes} = 4 - 2 - 1 = 1$$

So, $y = 1$.

Now, we find $w$, the number of angular nodes in the $4d_{z^2}$ orbital. The number of angular nodes for any orbital is simply equal to its azimuthal quantum number $l$.

Since it is a $d$ orbital, $l = 2$. (For a $d_{z^2}$ orbital, these angular nodes exist as two nodal cones).

So, $w = 2$.

Lastly, we need $q$, the number of nodal planes in the $3p_z$ orbital. The number of nodal planes is also given by the value of $l$.

For any $p$ orbital, $l = 1$. The $3p_z$ orbital has exactly one nodal plane (the $xy$-plane).

So, $q = 1$.

To find the final result, we add these four values together:

$$x + y + w + q = 6 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 10$$

q: The work function of sodium metal is $4.41 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}$. If photons of wavelength 300 nm are incident on the metal, the kinetic energy of the ejected electrons will be $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js} ; \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right) \times 10^{-21} \mathrm{~J}$.
question: The work function of sodium metal is $4.41 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}$. If photons of wavelength 300 nm are incident on the metal, the kinetic energy of the ejected electrons will be $\left(\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js} ; \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right) \times 10^{-21} \mathrm{~J}$.
solution:
Energy of an incident photon is

$$ E=\frac{hc}{\lambda} $$

Given,

$$ h=6.63\times 10^{-34}\ \text{Js} $$

$$ c=3\times 10^8\ \text{m s}^{-1} $$

$$ \lambda=300\ \text{nm}=300\times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}=3\times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} $$

So,

$$ E=\frac{6.63\times 10^{-34}\times 3\times 10^8}{3\times 10^{-7}} $$

$$ E=6.63\times 10^{-19}\ \text{J} $$

Work function of sodium metal is

$$ \phi=4.41\times 10^{-19}\ \text{J} $$

The kinetic energy of the ejected electron is

$$ K.E.=E-\phi $$

$$ K.E.=6.63\times 10^{-19}-4.41\times 10^{-19} $$

$$ K.E.=2.22\times 10^{-19}\ \text{J} $$

Writing this as $\times 10^{-21}\ \text{J}$,

$$ 2.22\times 10^{-19}=222\times 10^{-21}\ \text{J} $$

So, the kinetic energy of the ejected electrons will be

$$ \boxed{222\times 10^{-21}\ \text{J}} $$

A commercially sold conc. HCl is 35% HCl by mass. If the density of this commercial acid is 1.46 g/mL, the molarity of this solution is

(Atomic mass: Cl = 35.5 amu, H = 1 amu)
question:
A commercially sold conc. HCl is 35% HCl by mass. If the density of this commercial acid is 1.46 g/mL, the molarity of this solution is

(Atomic mass: Cl = 35.5 amu, H = 1 amu)
solution:
Given, density of commercial acid $=1.46 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$

To find molarity, we can take $1 \mathrm{~L}$ of the solution. Since $1 \mathrm{~L}=1000 \mathrm{~mL}$, the mass of this solution is:

So, mass of commercial acid $=$ Density × Volume

$$ =1.46 \times 1000=1460 \mathrm{~g} $$

The solution is $35 \%$ HCl by mass. This means $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of solution contains $35 \mathrm{~g}$ of HCl.

So, mass of HCl in $1460 \mathrm{~g}$ of solution is:

∴ Mass of given $35 \% \mathrm{HCl}=\frac{35}{100} \times 1460=511 \mathrm{~g}$

Molar mass of HCl $=1+35.5=36.5 \mathrm{~g/mol}$

Now, molarity is the number of moles of solute present in $1 \mathrm{~L}$ of solution.

$$ \text { Molarity of solution }=\frac{\text { Number of moles of acid }}{\text { Volume of acid (inlitre) }} $$

$$ =\frac{\text { Weight of acid }}{\text { M.M of acid × Volume of acid (in litre) }}$$

$$ =\frac{511}{36.5 \times 1 L}=14 M $$

q: From 8.50 g of a metal hydroxide, 0.1 mol of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ ions are produced. Determine the equivalent weight of the metal.
question: From 8.50 g of a metal hydroxide, 0.1 mol of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ ions are produced. Determine the equivalent weight of the metal.
solution:
For a metal hydroxide, the hydroxide part is $\mathrm{OH^-}$.

Given:

Mass of metal hydroxide $= 8.50 \text{ g}$

Amount of $\mathrm{OH^-}$ ions obtained $= 0.1 \text{ mol}$

Since $\mathrm{OH^-}$ is monovalent, $0.1$ mol of $\mathrm{OH^-}$ means $0.1$ equivalents of $\mathrm{OH^-}$.

So, the equivalent weight of the metal hydroxide is

$$ \text{Equivalent weight of hydroxide}=\frac{\text{mass}}{\text{number of equivalents}} $$

$$ =\frac{8.50}{0.1} $$

$$ =85 $$

Now, in a metal hydroxide,

$$ \text{Equivalent weight of metal hydroxide} = \text{Equivalent weight of metal}+\text{Equivalent weight of } \mathrm{OH} $$

The equivalent weight of $\mathrm{OH}$ is its molar mass, because its valency is $1$:

$$ \text{Equivalent weight of } \mathrm{OH}=16+1=17 $$

So,

$$ 85=\text{Equivalent weight of metal}+17 $$

$$ \text{Equivalent weight of metal}=85-17=68 $$

Thus, the equivalent weight of the metal is

$$ \boxed{68} $$

How many of the following statements are correct?

(1) Let $A = \{x : 1 < x < 2, \; x \text{ is a natural number}\}$. Then $A$ is the empty set, because there is no natural number between 1 and 2

(2) $B = \{x : x^2 - 2 = 0 \text{ and } x \text{ is rational number}\}$. Then $B$ is the empty set because the equation $x^2 - 2 = 0$ is not satisfied by any rational value of $x$

(4) $D = \{x : x^2 = 4, \; x \text{ is odd}\}$. Then $D$ is the empty set, because the equation $x^2 = 4$ is not satisfied by any odd value of $x$
question:
How many of the following statements are correct?

(1) Let $A = \{x : 1 < x < 2, \; x \text{ is a natural number}\}$. Then $A$ is the empty set, because there is no natural number between 1 and 2

(2) $B = \{x : x^2 - 2 = 0 \text{ and } x \text{ is rational number}\}$. Then $B$ is the empty set because the equation $x^2 - 2 = 0$ is not satisfied by any rational value of $x$

(3) $C = \{x : x \text{ is an even prime number greater than } 2\}$. Then $C$ is the empty set, because $2$ is the only even prime number

(4) $D = \{x : x^2 = 4, \; x \text{ is odd}\}$. Then $D$ is the empty set, because the equation $x^2 = 4$ is not satisfied by any odd value of $x$
solution:
All the four statements are correct.

For statement (1),

$$A=\{x:1 < x < 2,\ x \text{ is a natural number}\}$$

Natural numbers are $1,2,3,\ldots$.

There is no natural number strictly between $1$ and $2$.

So, $A$ is the empty set.

Statement (1) is correct.

For statement (2),

$$B=\{x:x^2-2=0,\ x \text{ is rational}\}$$

From the equation,

$$x^2-2=0$$

$$x^2=2$$

$$x=\pm \sqrt{2}$$

But $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational, so neither $\sqrt{2}$ nor $-\sqrt{2}$ is rational.

So, $B$ is the empty set.

Statement (2) is correct.

For statement (3),

$$C=\{x:x \text{ is an even prime number greater than }2\}$$

The only even prime number is $2$.

There is no even prime number greater than $2$.

So, $C$ is the empty set.

Statement (3) is correct.

For statement (4),

$$D=\{x:x^2=4,\ x \text{ is odd}\}$$

From the equation,

$$x^2=4$$

$$x=\pm 2$$

Both $2$ and $-2$ are even, not odd.

So, $D$ is the empty set.

Statement (4) is correct.

Hence, the number of correct statements is

$$4$$

So the correct option is Option D.

q: If equations $x^2 - 5x + 5 = 0$ and $x^3 + a x^2 + b x + 5 = 0$ have common root, then value of $a + b$ ($a, b \in \mathbb{Q}$) is
question: If equations $x^2 - 5x + 5 = 0$ and $x^3 + a x^2 + b x + 5 = 0$ have common root, then value of $a + b$ ($a, b \in \mathbb{Q}$) is
solution:
We start by looking at the first equation, which is $x^2 - 5x + 5 = 0$.

$$D = (-5)^2 - 4(1)(5) = 25 - 20 = 5$$

Since the discriminant is $5$, which is not a perfect square, the roots of this quadratic equation are irrational.

We are given that the second equation, $x^3 + ax^2 + bx + 5 = 0$, has rational coefficients because $a$ and $b$ are rational numbers ($a, b \in \mathbb{Q}$). A key property of polynomials with rational coefficients is that if they have an irrational root, its conjugate must also be a root.

Because the two equations share a common root and the root is irrational, its conjugate must also be a common root. This means both roots of the quadratic equation are also roots of the cubic equation.

Since both roots match, the quadratic expression $x^2 - 5x + 5$ must be a factor of the cubic expression $x^3 + ax^2 + bx + 5$.

Let the third root of the cubic equation be $\alpha$.

$$x^3 + ax^2 + bx + 5 = (x^2 - 5x + 5)(x - \alpha)$$

$$=(x^2 - 5x + 5)(x - \alpha) = x^3 - \alpha x^2 - 5x^2 + 5\alpha x + 5x - 5\alpha$$

$$= x^3 - (\alpha + 5)x^2 + (5\alpha + 5)x - 5\alpha$$

By comparing this expanded form with our original cubic equation $x^3 + ax^2 + bx + 5 = 0$, we can easily find the value of $\alpha$ by looking at the constant terms.

$$-5\alpha = 5$$

$$\alpha = -1$$

Comparing the coefficients of $x^2$:

$$a = -(\alpha + 5)$$

$$a = -(-1 + 5) = -4$$

Comparing the coefficients of $x$:

$$b = 5\alpha + 5$$

$$b = 5(-1) + 5 = 0$$

$$\therefore $$ $$a + b = -4 + 0 = -4$$

The correct option is Option B.

How many of the following statements are sets.

(i) The collection of all the months of a year beginning with the letter J

(ii) The collection of ten most talented writers of India

(iii) A team of eleven best-cricket batsmen of the world

(iv) The collection of all boys in your class

question:
How many of the following statements are sets.

(i) The collection of all the months of a year beginning with the letter J

(ii) The collection of ten most talented writers of India

(iii) A team of eleven best-cricket batsmen of the world

(iv) The collection of all boys in your class

(v) The collection of all natural numbers less than 100
solution:
To decide whether a collection is a set, we need to check one simple thing — the objects in the collection must be well-defined. This means, given any object, we should be able to say clearly whether it belongs to the collection or not.

Let’s look at each one.

(i) Months of a year beginning with the letter J — these are January, June and July. Clearly defined, no confusion. This is a set.

(ii) Ten most talented writers of India — the word talented depends on personal opinion. Different people will choose different writers. Not well-defined, so this is not a set.

(iii) A team of eleven best cricket batsmen of the world — again, best is not well-defined. Opinions differ. Not a set.

(iv) All boys in your class — this is perfectly clear. We can list them exactly. This is a set.

(v) All natural numbers less than 100 — these are $1, 2, 3, \dots, 99$. Clearly defined. This is a set.

So the collections that form sets are (i), (iv) and (v) — that makes 3 sets.

The correct option is C.

q: If $2^a = 3$ and $9^b = 4$ then value of $(ab)$ is
question: If $2^a = 3$ and $9^b = 4$ then value of $(ab)$ is
solution:
Given,

$$2^a=3 \quad \text{and} \quad 9^b=4$$

We need to find $ab$.

From $2^a=3$, take logarithm on both sides:

$$a=\log_2 3$$

Now from $9^b=4$,

$$(3^2)^b=2^2$$

$$3^{2b}=2^2$$

Taking logarithm base $3$,

$$2b=\log_3 4$$

$$2b=2\log_3 2$$

$$b=\log_3 2$$

Now,

$$ab=(\log_2 3)(\log_3 2)$$

Using the property,

$$\log_2 3 \cdot \log_3 2=1$$

Hence,

$$ab=1$$

So the correct option is A, that is, $1$.

question: Let S be the set of all real numbers. Then, the relation $R = \{(a, b) : 1 + ab > 0\}$ on S is
solution:
For the relation

$$ R=\{(a,b):1+ab>0\} $$

on the set of all real numbers, we check reflexive, symmetric and transitive properties.

For reflexive, we need $(a,a)\in R$ for every real number $a$.

Now,

$$ 1+a\cdot a=1+a^2 $$

Since $a^2\ge 0$ for every real number $a$,

$$ 1+a^2>0 $$

So $(a,a)\in R$ for every real number $a$.

Hence, $R$ is reflexive.

For symmetric, suppose $(a,b)\in R$.

Then,

$$ 1+ab>0 $$

But $ab=ba$, so

$$ 1+ba>0 $$

This means $(b,a)\in R$.

Hence, $R$ is symmetric.

Now check transitive. For transitive, if $(a,b)\in R$ and $(b,c)\in R$, then $(a,c)$ must also be in $R$.

Take

$$ a=1,\quad b=0,\quad c=-2 $$

Now,

$$ 1+ab=1+(1)(0)=1>0 $$

So $(1,0)\in R$.

Also,

$$ 1+bc=1+(0)(-2)=1>0 $$

So $(0,-2)\in R$.

But,

$$ 1+ac=1+(1)(-2)=-1 $$

which is not greater than $0$.

So $(1,-2)\notin R$.

Hence, $R$ is not transitive.

So the relation is reflexive and symmetric but not transitive.

Correct option: Option A.

question: $\log_{10}(\log_2 3) + \log_{10}(\log_3 4) + \log_{10}(\log_4 5) + \ldots + \log_{10}(\log_{1023} 1024)$ simplifies to
solution:
$$\log_{10}(\log_2 3) + \log_{10}(\log_3 4) + \ldots + \log_{10}(\log_{1023} 1024)$$

$$= \log_{10}\left(\log_2 3 \cdot \log_3 4 \cdot \log_4 5 \cdots \log_{1023} 1024\right)$$

$$\log_2 3 = \frac{\ln 3}{\ln 2}, \quad \log_3 4 = \frac{\ln 4}{\ln 3}, \quad \log_4 5 = \frac{\ln 5}{\ln 4}, \ldots$$

When we multiply these :

$$\frac{\ln 3}{\ln 2} \cdot \frac{\ln 4}{\ln 3} \cdot \frac{\ln 5}{\ln 4} \cdots \frac{\ln 1024}{\ln 1023} = \frac{\ln 1024}{\ln 2}$$

So the product simplifies to $\log_2 1024$.

Since $1024 = 2^{10}$, we get $\log_2 1024 = 10$.

So the whole expression becomes:

$$\log_{10} 10 = 1$$

The value is $1$, which is an integer (but not prime, not composite).

The correct option is D — an integer.

q: Let $R = \{(1, 2), (2, 3)\}$ be a relation defined on set $\{1, 2, 3\}$. The minimum number of ordered pairs required to be added in $R$, such that enlarged relation becomes an equivalence relation is
question: Let $R = \{(1, 2), (2, 3)\}$ be a relation defined on set $\{1, 2, 3\}$. The minimum number of ordered pairs required to be added in $R$, such that enlarged relation becomes an equivalence relation is
solution:
For $R$ to be an equivalence relation on $\{1, 2, 3\}$, it must be reflexive, symmetric and transitive.

The given relation is $R = \{(1,2), (2,3)\}$.

For reflexivity, we need $(1,1), (2,2), (3,3)$. So we add these three pairs.

For symmetry, since $(1,2) \in R$, we need $(2,1)$. Since $(2,3) \in R$, we need $(3,2)$. So we add $(2,1)$ and $(3,2)$.

Now check transitivity. We have $(1,2)$ and $(2,3)$, so we need $(1,3)$. Also $(3,2)$ and $(2,1)$ gives us the need for $(3,1)$.

Once $(1,3)$ is added, by symmetry $(3,1)$ is also needed (which we already noted).

So the pairs to be added are:

$$(1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (2,1), (3,2), (1,3), (3,1)$$

That makes $7$ ordered pairs.

The correct option is C.

Given $\log_3 a = p = \log_b c$ and $\log_b 9 = \frac{2}{p^2}$. If

$ \log_9 \left( \frac{a^4 b^3}{c} \right) = \alpha p^3 + \beta p^2 + \gamma p + \delta \quad (\forall p \in \mathbb{R} - \{0\}) $

then $(\alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta)$ equals :
question:
Given $\log_3 a = p = \log_b c$ and $\log_b 9 = \frac{2}{p^2}$. If

$ \log_9 \left( \frac{a^4 b^3}{c} \right) = \alpha p^3 + \beta p^2 + \gamma p + \delta \quad (\forall p \in \mathbb{R} - \{0\}) $

then $(\alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta)$ equals :
solution:
We have $\log_3 a = p$, so $a = 3^p$.

Also $\log_b 9 = \dfrac{2}{p^2}$, which means $b^{2/p^2} = 9 = 3^2$.

So $b^{1/p^2} = 3$, giving us $b = 3^{p^2}$.

Now $\log_b c = p$ means $c = b^p = 3^{p^3}$.

$$\therefore $$ $$\frac{a^4 b^3}{c} = \frac{3^{4p} \cdot 3^{3p^2}}{3^{p^3}} = 3^{4p + 3p^2 - p^3}$$

$$\log_9\left(\frac{a^4 b^3}{c}\right) = \log_9 3^{4p + 3p^2 - p^3} = \frac{4p + 3p^2 - p^3}{2}$$

This gives:

$$\log_9\left(\frac{a^4 b^3}{c}\right) = -\frac{1}{2}p^3 + \frac{3}{2}p^2 + 2p + 0$$

Comparing with $\alpha p^3 + \beta p^2 + \gamma p + \delta$:

$$\alpha = -\tfrac{1}{2},\quad \beta = \tfrac{3}{2},\quad \gamma = 2,\quad \delta = 0$$

Adding them up:

$$\alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta = -\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{3}{2} + 2 + 0 = 3$$

The correct answer is Option C: $3$.

q:
How many of the following statements are correct.

P : All infinite sets cannot be described in the roster form.

Q : Let W be the set of the days of the week. Then W is finite.

R : Let $A = \{x : 1 < x < 2, x \text{ is a natural number}\}$. Then A is the empty set.

S : Let G be the set of points on a line. Then G is infinite.
question:
How many of the following statements are correct.

P : All infinite sets cannot be described in the roster form.

Q : Let W be the set of the days of the week. Then W is finite.

R : Let $A = \{x : 1 < x < 2, x \text{ is a natural number}\}$. Then A is the empty set.

S : Let G be the set of points on a line. Then G is infinite.
solution:
All four statements are correct.

P: Infinite sets like the set of natural numbers can sometimes be shown in roster form using dots (e.g., $\{1,2,3,\ldots\}$), but sets like the set of real numbers between 0 and 1 cannot be written in roster form at all. So it is true that all infinite sets cannot be described in roster form.

Q: The days of the week are only seven — Monday, Tuesday, …, Sunday. Since the number of elements is countable and limited, $W$ is finite. Correct.

R: We need natural numbers $x$ such that $1 < x < 2$. There is no natural number strictly between 1 and 2. So $A = \varnothing$, the empty set. Correct.

S: A line contains infinitely many points. So the set $G$ of points on a line is infinite. Correct.

Since all four statements are correct, the answer is Option A: 4.

q: If the roots of the equation $x^2 - 5x + 16 = 0$ are $\alpha, \beta$ and the roots of the equation $x^2 + px + q = 0$ are $(\alpha^2 + \beta^2)$ and $\frac{\alpha\beta}{2}$, then
question: If the roots of the equation $x^2 - 5x + 16 = 0$ are $\alpha, \beta$ and the roots of the equation $x^2 + px + q = 0$ are $(\alpha^2 + \beta^2)$ and $\frac{\alpha\beta}{2}$, then
solution:
For the equation

$$x^2 - 5x + 16 = 0$$

the roots are $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

Using the NCERT relation between roots and coefficients:

$$\alpha + \beta = 5$$

and

$$\alpha\beta = 16$$

Now the roots of the second equation are

$$\alpha^2 + \beta^2$$

and

$$\frac{\alpha\beta}{2}$$

First find $\alpha^2 + \beta^2$:

$$\alpha^2 + \beta^2 = (\alpha + \beta)^2 - 2\alpha\beta$$

$$= 5^2 - 2(16)$$

$$= 25 - 32$$

$$= -7$$

Also,

$$\frac{\alpha\beta}{2} = \frac{16}{2} = 8$$

So the roots of

$$x^2 + px + q = 0$$

are $-7$ and $8$.

For a quadratic equation $x^2 + px + q = 0$,

sum of roots $= -p$ and product of roots $= q$.

So,

$$-p = -7 + 8 = 1$$

$$p = -1$$

And,

$$q = (-7)(8) = -56$$

So,

$$p = -1,\quad q = -56$$

Correct option: Option D.

q: The greatest value of $(4 \log_{10} x - \log_{x} (0.0001))$ for $0 < x < 1$ is
question: The greatest value of $(4 \log_{10} x - \log_{x} (0.0001))$ for $0 < x < 1$ is
solution:
$$E = 4 \log_{10} x - \log_{x} (0.0001)$$

$$E = 4 \log_{10} x - \log_{x} (10^{-4})$$

$$E = 4 \log_{10} x - (-4) \log_{x} 10$$

$$E = 4 \log_{10} x + 4 \log_{x} 10$$

$$E = 4 \log_{10} x + \frac{4}{\log_{10} x}$$

$$E = 4 \left( \log_{10} x + \frac{1}{\log_{10} x} \right)$$

We are given the condition that $0 < x < 1$. For any positive number less than $1$, its logarithm to the base $10$ is always negative.

Let $\log_{10} x = t$, where $t$ is a negative real number ($t < 0$).

Our expression simplifies to:

$$E = 4 \left(t + \frac{1}{t}\right)$$

Let's see why this is true. For any real number $t$, the square of $(t + 1)$ is always non-negative:

$$(t + 1)^2 \ge 0$$

$$t^2 + 2t + 1 \ge 0$$

$$t^2 + 1 \ge -2t$$

$$\frac{t^2 + 1}{t} \le -2$$

$$t + \frac{1}{t} \le -2$$

The maximum possible value for $t + \frac{1}{t}$ is $-2$, which occurs when $t = -1$.

Substitute this maximum value back into our expression for $E$:

$$E \le 4 (-2)$$

$$E \le -8$$

The greatest value of the given expression is $-8$.

Option D is correct.

q: The quadratic $x^2 + ax + b + 1 = 0$ has roots which are positive integers, then $(a^2 + b^2)$ can be equal to :
question: The quadratic $x^2 + ax + b + 1 = 0$ has roots which are positive integers, then $(a^2 + b^2)$ can be equal to :
solution:
Let the two positive integer roots be $p$ and $q$.

From the quadratic $x^2 + ax + b + 1 = 0$, by comparing with sum and product of roots:

$$p + q = -a$$

$$pq = b + 1$$

So $a = -(p+q)$ and $b = pq - 1$.

Now,

$$a^2 + b^2 = (p+q)^2 + (pq - 1)^2$$

If $p = 1, q = 1$:

$$a^2 + b^2 = 4 + 0 = 4$$

If $p = 1, q = 2$:

$$a^2 + b^2 = 9 + 1 = 10$$

If $p = 1, q = 3$:

$$a^2 + b^2 = 16 + 4 = 20$$

If $p = 2, q = 2$:

$$a^2 + b^2 = 16 + 9 = 25$$

If $p = 1, q = 4$:

$$a^2 + b^2 = 25 + 9 = 34$$

If $p = 2, q = 3$:

$$a^2 + b^2 = 25 + 25 = 50$$

That matches option A.

If $p = 1, q = 5$: $36 + 16 = 52$

If $p = 2, q = 4$: $36 + 49 = 85$

If $p = 3, q = 3$: $36 + 64 = 100$

If $p = 1, q = 6$: $49 + 25 = 74$

So among the given options, only $50$ is achievable (when the roots are $2$ and $3$).

The correct answer is Option A: 50.

q: For real numbers $x$ and $y$ define a relation $R$, $xRy$ iff $|x| = |y|$, the relation $R$ is
question: For real numbers $x$ and $y$ define a relation $R$, $xRy$ iff $|x| = |y|$, the relation $R$ is
solution:
For real numbers $x$ and $y$, the relation $R$ is defined as

$$ xRy \iff |x| = |y| $$

We check whether it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.

For reflexive:

Every real number $x$ has the same modulus as itself.

$$ |x| = |x| $$

So, $xRx$ for every real number $x$.

Hence, $R$ is reflexive.

For symmetric:

If $xRy$, then

$$ |x| = |y| $$

This also means

$$ |y| = |x| $$

So, $yRx$.

Hence, $R$ is symmetric.

For transitive:

If $xRy$ and $yRz$, then

$$ |x| = |y| $$

and

$$ |y| = |z| $$

So,

$$ |x| = |z| $$

Hence, $xRz$.

So, $R$ is transitive.

Since the relation is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, it is an equivalence relation.

The correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D: Equivalence}} $$

q: Let $A = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and let $B = A \times A$. Define the relation $R$ on $B$ as follows: $(a, b)\;R\;(c, d)$ if and only if $ad = cb$. Then $R$ is
question: Let $A = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and let $B = A \times A$. Define the relation $R$ on $B$ as follows: $(a, b)\;R\;(c, d)$ if and only if $ad = cb$. Then $R$ is
solution:
Let's check each property of the relation $R$ on $B = A \times A$, where $(a,b)\,R\,(c,d)$ means $ad = cb$.

Reflexive: For any $(a,b) \in B$, we need $(a,b)\,R\,(a,b)$, which means $ab = ba$. This is always true. So $R$ is reflexive.

Symmetric: Suppose $(a,b)\,R\,(c,d)$, so $ad = cb$. We need to check whether $(c,d)\,R\,(a,b)$, which means $cb = ad$. This is the same equation, just written the other way. So $R$ is symmetric.

Transitive: Suppose $(a,b)\,R\,(c,d)$ and $(c,d)\,R\,(e,f)$. Then

$$ad = cb \quad \text{and} \quad cf = ed.$$

From the first, $\dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{c}{d}$, and from the second, $\dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{e}{f}$. So $\dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{e}{f}$, which gives $af = eb$. That means $(a,b)\,R\,(e,f)$. So $R$ is transitive.

Since $R$ is reflexive, symmetric and transitive, it is an equivalence relation.

The correct option is D.

q: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of the equation $(x - a)(x - b) = c$ and $c \neq 0$, then roots of the equation $(x - \alpha)(x - \beta) + c = 0$ are
question: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of the equation $(x - a)(x - b) = c$ and $c \neq 0$, then roots of the equation $(x - \alpha)(x - \beta) + c = 0$ are
solution:
Since $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of $(x-a)(x-b) = c$, we can rewrite this equation as

$$(x-a)(x-b) - c = 0.$$

This means the quadratic expression $(x-a)(x-b) - c$ has $\alpha$ and $\beta$ as its roots. So it can be factorised as

$$(x-a)(x-b) - c = (x-\alpha)(x-\beta).$$

$$(x-a)(x-b) = (x-\alpha)(x-\beta) + c.$$

Look carefully at the right side. It is exactly the expression whose roots we want to find. So

$$(x-\alpha)(x-\beta) + c = (x-a)(x-b).$$

Setting this equal to zero gives $(x-a)(x-b) = 0$, whose roots are clearly $a$ and $b$.

So the correct option is C: $a$ and $b$.

q:
Statement-1: If sum of the roots of quadratic equation $2x^2 + bx + c = 0$ is equal to sum of the squares of the roots, then $b^2 + 2b = 4c$.

Statement-2: If one root of quadratic equation $x^2 + px + q = 0$, is $4 + \sqrt{3}$, then other root is $4 - \sqrt{3}$.

Which of the following is correct?
question:
Statement-1: If sum of the roots of quadratic equation $2x^2 + bx + c = 0$ is equal to sum of the squares of the roots, then $b^2 + 2b = 4c$.

Statement-2: If one root of quadratic equation $x^2 + px + q = 0$, is $4 + \sqrt{3}$, then other root is $4 - \sqrt{3}$.

Which of the following is correct?
options: Statement-1 is true and Statement-2 is false
Statement-1 is false and Statement-2 is true
solution:
For Statement-1, suppose the roots of

$$2x^2+bx+c=0$$

are $\alpha$ and $\beta$.

Using the relation between roots and coefficients,

$$\alpha+\beta=\frac{-b}{2}$$

and

$$\alpha\beta=\frac{c}{2}$$

Also,

$$\alpha^2+\beta^2=(\alpha+\beta)^2-2\alpha\beta$$

So,

$$\alpha^2+\beta^2=\left(\frac{-b}{2}\right)^2-2\left(\frac{c}{2}\right)$$

$$\alpha^2+\beta^2=\frac{b^2}{4}-c$$

Given that the sum of roots is equal to the sum of squares of roots,

$$\frac{-b}{2}=\frac{b^2}{4}-c$$

$$-2b=b^2-4c$$

$$b^2+2b=4c$$

So, Statement-1 is true.

For Statement-2, the equation is

$$x^2+px+q=0$$

One root is given as $4+\sqrt{3}$.

The other root will be $4-\sqrt{3}$ only if $p$ and $q$ are rational. But the statement does not say that $p$ and $q$ are rational.

For example, the other root could be $2$. Then the equation would still be a quadratic equation of the form $x^2+px+q=0$, but the other root is not $4-\sqrt{3}$.

So, Statement-2 is false.

Hence, the correct option is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option A}}$$

q: If the roots of equation $(4p - p^2 - 5)x^2 - (2p - 1)x + 3p = 0$ lie on either side of unity, then the number of integral values of $p$ is
question: If the roots of equation $(4p - p^2 - 5)x^2 - (2p - 1)x + 3p = 0$ lie on either side of unity, then the number of integral values of $p$ is
solution:
For the roots to lie on either side of unity, the value of the quadratic at $x = 1$ must have the opposite sign to the coefficient of $x^2$. In other words, $a \cdot f(1) < 0$.

First look at the coefficient of $x^2$:

$$a = 4p - p^2 - 5 = -(p^2 - 4p + 5) = -((p-2)^2 + 1)$$

Since $(p-2)^2 + 1$ is always positive, $a$ is always negative for every real $p$.

Now let's find $f(1)$:

$$f(1) = (4p - p^2 - 5) - (2p - 1) + 3p = 4p - p^2 - 5 - 2p + 1 + 3p = -p^2 + 5p - 4$$

So $f(1) = -(p^2 - 5p + 4) = -(p-1)(p-4)$.

The condition $a \cdot f(1) < 0$ becomes:

$$[-(p^2 - 4p + 5)] \cdot [-(p-1)(p-4)] < 0$$

$$(p^2 - 4p + 5)(p-1)(p-4) < 0$$

Since $p^2 - 4p + 5 > 0$ always, this reduces to:

$$(p-1)(p-4) < 0$$

This gives $1 < p < 4$.

The integral values are $p = 2$ and $p = 3$, so there are $2$ integral values.

The answer is option B.

q: If exactly one root of the equation $2^k x^2 - 4^k x + 2^k - 1 = 0$ lies in $[0,1)$, then complete range of $k$ is
question: If exactly one root of the equation $2^k x^2 - 4^k x + 2^k - 1 = 0$ lies in $[0,1)$, then complete range of $k$ is
solution:
$$f(x) = 2^k x^2 - 4^k x + 2^k - 1$$

The leading coefficient of this quadratic equation is $2^k$. Since $2^k > 0$ for all real numbers $k$, the graph of $f(x)$ is a parabola that always opens upwards.

For a quadratic equation to have exactly one root in the open interval $(0, 1)$, the values of the function at the endpoints of the interval must have opposite signs. This means $f(0) \cdot f(1) < 0$. Let's evaluate $f(x)$ at the endpoints $x = 0$ and $x = 1$.

At $x = 0$:

$$f(0) = 2^k (0)^2 - 4^k (0) + 2^k - 1 = 2^k - 1$$

At $x = 1$:

$$f(1) = 2^k (1)^2 - 4^k (1) + 2^k - 1$$

$$f(1) = 2 \cdot 2^k - (2^k)^2 - 1$$

Rewriting this, we get:

$$f(1) = - ((2^k)^2 - 2 \cdot 2^k + 1)$$

$$f(1) = - (2^k - 1)^2$$

Now, we check the condition $f(0) \cdot f(1) < 0$:

$$(2^k - 1) \cdot (- (2^k - 1)^2) < 0$$

$$- (2^k - 1)^3 < 0$$

$$(2^k - 1)^3 > 0$$

$$2^k - 1 > 0$$

$$2^k > 1$$

$$k > 0$$

For $k > 0$, exactly one root of the equation lies in the open interval $(0, 1)$. Because neither $f(0)$ nor $f(1)$ is equal to zero here, none of the roots are exactly $0$ or $1$. Thus, exactly one root lies in the interval $[0, 1)$.

If $k = 0$, we can substitute it directly into the original equation:

$$2^0 x^2 - 4^0 x + 2^0 - 1 = 0$$

$$1 \cdot x^2 - 1 \cdot x + 1 - 1 = 0$$

$$x^2 - x = 0$$

$$x(x - 1) = 0$$

The roots are $x = 0$ and $x = 1$.

Looking at our required interval $[0, 1)$, the root $x = 0$ is included, but the root $x = 1$ is not. This means for $k=0$, exactly one root lies in the required interval. Therefore, $k = 0$ is also a valid part of the range.

Finally, let us check for $k < 0$.

When $k < 0$, $2^k < 1$, which makes $f(0) = 2^k - 1 < 0$.

Also, for any $k \neq 0$, $f(1) = -(2^k - 1)^2$ is strictly negative.

Notice that the product of the roots is given by $\frac{c}{a} = \frac{2^k - 1}{2^k}$. For $k < 0$, the numerator is negative and the denominator is positive, so the product of the roots is negative. This tells us one root is negative and the other is positive.

Since $f(1) < 0$ and the parabola opens upwards, the function will only cross the x-axis again at a value strictly greater than $1$. Hence, for $k < 0$, one root is less than $0$ and the other is greater than $1$, meaning no roots lie in $[0, 1)$.

Combining all our valid conditions, the requirement is satisfied for all $k \geq 0$.

The complete range of $k$ is $[0, \infty)$.

Option D

q: Find the sum of all possible greatest integer values of $x, x \in R$ and $x$ satisfies the equation $x^2-x-1=2^x-\log _2\left(x^2+2^x\right)$
question: Find the sum of all possible greatest integer values of $x, x \in R$ and $x$ satisfies the equation $x^2-x-1=2^x-\log _2\left(x^2+2^x\right)$
solution:
We are given the equation:

$$x^2 - x - 1 = 2^x - \log_2(x^2 + 2^x)$$

$$x^2 + \log_2(x^2 + 2^x) = 2^x + x + 1$$

$$x^2 + 2^x + \log_2(x^2 + 2^x) = 2^x + 2^x + x + 1$$

$$x^2 + 2^x + \log_2(x^2 + 2^x) = 2 \cdot 2^x + x + 1$$

$$x^2 + 2^x + \log_2(x^2 + 2^x) = 2^{x+1} + x + 1$$

$$x^2 + 2^x + \log_2(x^2 + 2^x) = 2^{x+1} + \log_2(2^{x+1})$$

Now, consider the function $f(t) = t + \log_2 t$, which is defined for $t > 0$. Using this function, our equation can be rewritten simply as:

$$f(x^2 + 2^x) = f(2^{x+1})$$

The function $f(t)$ is the sum of two strictly increasing functions representing $t$ and $\log_2 t$. Because of this, $f(t)$ itself is strictly increasing and therefore one-one (injective). This tells us that if $f(a) = f(b)$, then the inputs must identically be equal, meaning $a = b$.

Equating the inputs, we get:

$$x^2 + 2^x = 2^{x+1}$$

$$x^2 = 2^{x+1} - 2^x$$

$$x^2 = 2^x (2 - 1)$$

$$x^2 = 2^x$$

(Note that for any satisfying value of $x$, we have $x^2 + 2^x = 2^{x+1}$, which is always strictly positive. So, the domain condition for the original logarithm is naturally satisfied for our solutions.)

We need to find all real solutions for the simplified equation $x^2 = 2^x$.

If $x = 2$, we have $2^2 = 2^2 \implies 4 = 4$

If $x = 4$, we have $4^2 = 2^4 \implies 16 = 16$

To see if there are any negative real roots, let's define a function $g(x) = x^2 - 2^x$ and check its values near zero:

At $x = 0$, $g(0) = 0 - 1 = -1$ (which is negative)

At $x = -1$, $g(-1) = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$ (which is positive)

Since $g(x)$ is a continuous function and changes sign between $-1$ and $0$, the Intermediate Value Theorem confirms there is a root in the interval $(-1, 0)$. Let's call this root $\alpha$. These make up all three real solutions to this equation.

We are asked to find the sum of all possible greatest integer values of $x$. This means we need to evaluate the greatest integer function $[x]$ for each of the roots we found and add them up.

For $x = \alpha$ where $-1 < \alpha < 0$, its greatest integer value is $[\alpha] = -1$.

For $x = 2$, its greatest integer value is $[2] = 2$.

For $x = 4$, its greatest integer value is $[4] = 4$.

The sum of these greatest integer values is:

$$-1 + 2 + 4 = 5$$

q: Let $\mathrm{S}=\{\mathrm{a} \mid \mathrm{a} \in \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{a} \leq 18\}$. Define two relations $\mathrm{R}_1$ and $\mathrm{R}_2$ as sets of ordered pairs by $\mathrm{R}_1=\{(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}) \mid \mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y} \in \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{y}=3\mathrm{x}\}$ and $\mathrm{R}_2=\left\{(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}) \mid \mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y} \in \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x}^2\right\}$. Determine the number of elements in the set $\mathrm{R}_1 \backslash \left(\mathrm{R}_1 \cap \mathrm{R}_2\right)$.
question: Let $\mathrm{S}=\{\mathrm{a} \mid \mathrm{a} \in \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{a} \leq 18\}$. Define two relations $\mathrm{R}_1$ and $\mathrm{R}_2$ as sets of ordered pairs by $\mathrm{R}_1=\{(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}) \mid \mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y} \in \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{y}=3\mathrm{x}\}$ and $\mathrm{R}_2=\left\{(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}) \mid \mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y} \in \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x}^2\right\}$. Determine the number of elements in the set $\mathrm{R}_1 \backslash \left(\mathrm{R}_1 \cap \mathrm{R}_2\right)$.
solution:
The set $S$ consists of all natural numbers up to 18.

$$S = \{1, 2, 3, \dots, 18\}$$

For the relation $R_1$, the given condition is $y = 3x$, where both $x$ and $y$ must belong to $S$.

If $x = 1$, $y = 3(1) = 3$. The pair is $(1, 3)$.

If $x = 2$, $y = 3(2) = 6$. The pair is $(2, 6)$.

If $x = 3$, $y = 3(3) = 9$. The pair is $(3, 9)$.

If $x = 4$, $y = 3(4) = 12$. The pair is $(4, 12)$.

If $x = 5$, $y = 3(5) = 15$. The pair is $(5, 15)$.

If $x = 6$, $y = 3(6) = 18$. The pair is $(6, 18)$.

If $x = 7$, $y = 3(7) = 21$. This value is not in $S$ since the maximum number in $S$ is 18. So, we stop here.

Writing this as a set of ordered pairs, we have:

$$R_1 = \{(1, 3), (2, 6), (3, 9), (4, 12), (5, 15), (6, 18)\}$$

Next, we look at the relation $R_2$. The condition here is $y = x^2$, with both $x$ and $y$ in $S$. Let us do the same and substitute values for $x$:

If $x = 1$, $y = 1^2 = 1$. The pair is $(1, 1)$.

If $x = 2$, $y = 2^2 = 4$. The pair is $(2, 4)$.

If $x = 3$, $y = 3^2 = 9$. The pair is $(3, 9)$.

If $x = 4$, $y = 4^2 = 16$. The pair is $(4, 16)$.

If $x = 5$, $y = 5^2 = 25$. This is greater than 18, so it is not in $S$.

This gives us the set:

$$R_2 = \{(1, 1), (2, 4), (3, 9), (4, 16)\}$$

$$R_1 \cap R_2 = \{(3, 9)\}$$

We want to determine the number of elements in the set $R_1 \setminus (R_1 \cap R_2)$. This notation means we take the set $R_1$ and remove any elements that belong to $R_1 \cap R_2$.

By taking out the pair $(3, 9)$ from $R_1$, we get the following set:

$$R_1 \setminus (R_1 \cap R_2) = \{(1, 3), (2, 6), (4, 12), (5, 15), (6, 18)\}$$

Counting the ordered pairs in this final set, we see there are exactly 5 elements. The number of elements in the set $R_1 \setminus (R_1 \cap R_2)$ is 5.

q: Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of $x^2+\sqrt{3} x-16=0$ and $\gamma$ and $\delta$ be the roots of $x^2+3 x-1=0$. If $P_n=\alpha^n+\beta^n, Q_n=\gamma^n+\delta^n \quad(n \in N)$ then $3\left(\frac{P_{26}+\sqrt{3} P_{25}}{8 P_{24}}\right)-6\left(\frac{Q_{26}-Q_{24}}{Q_{25}}\right)=$ $\_\_\_\_$
question: Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the roots of $x^2+\sqrt{3} x-16=0$ and $\gamma$ and $\delta$ be the roots of $x^2+3 x-1=0$. If $P_n=\alpha^n+\beta^n, Q_n=\gamma^n+\delta^n \quad(n \in N)$ then $3\left(\frac{P_{26}+\sqrt{3} P_{25}}{8 P_{24}}\right)-6\left(\frac{Q_{26}-Q_{24}}{Q_{25}}\right)=$ $\_\_\_\_$
solution:
First equation is

$$x^2 + \sqrt{3}x - 16 = 0$$ ....(i)

$\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of first equation

$$\Rightarrow \alpha^2 + \sqrt{3}\alpha - 16 = 0$$ .......(ii)

$$\alpha^2 + \sqrt{3}\alpha = 16 \qquad \text{--- (iii)}$$

$$\beta^2 + \sqrt{3}\beta - 16 = 0 \qquad \text{--- (iv)}$$

$$\beta^2 + \sqrt{3}\beta = 16$$

Given: $P_n = \alpha^n + \beta^n$

Multiplying (iii) by $\alpha^{24}$ and (iv) by $\beta^{24}$

$$\alpha^{26} + \sqrt{3}\alpha^{25} = 16\alpha^{24}$$

$$\beta^{26} + \sqrt{3}\beta^{25} = 16\beta^{24}$$

Adding the two given equations,

$$\left(\alpha^{26} + \beta^{26}\right) + \sqrt{3}\left(\alpha^{25} + \beta^{25}\right) = 16\left(\alpha^{24} + \beta^{24}\right).$$

Using

$$P_n = \alpha^n + \beta^n,$$

we obtain

$$P_{26} + \sqrt{3}P_{25} = 16P_{24}.$$

Therefore,

$$\frac{P_{26} + \sqrt{3}P_{25}}{8P_{24}} = \frac{16P_{24}}{8P_{24}}$$

$$= 2.$$

Hence

$$3\left(\frac{P_{26} + \sqrt{3}P_{25}}{8P_{24}}\right) = 3 \times 2 = 6.$$

Now consider

$$x^2 + 3x - 1 = 0.$$

Its roots are $\gamma, \delta$.

Then

$$\gamma^2 + 3\gamma - 1 = 0,$$

so

$$\gamma^2 - 1 = -3\gamma. ..... (v)$$

Similarly,

$$\delta^2 + 3\delta - 1 = 0,$$

so

$$\delta^2 - 1 = -3\delta. ....... (vi)$$

given

$$Q_n = \gamma^n + \delta^n.$$

Multiplying (v) by $\gamma^{24}$,

$$\gamma^{26} - \gamma^{24} = -3\gamma^{25}.$$

Multiplying (vi) by $\delta^{24}$,

$$\delta^{26} - \delta^{24} = -3\delta^{25}.$$

Adding,

$$(\gamma^{26} + \delta^{26}) - (\gamma^{24} + \delta^{24}) = -3(\gamma^{25} + \delta^{25}).$$

Hence

$$Q_{26} - Q_{24} = -3Q_{25}.$$

Therefore,

$$\frac{Q_{26} - Q_{24}}{Q_{25}} = -3.$$

Thus

$$-6\left(\frac{Q_{26} - Q_{24}}{Q_{25}}\right) = -6(-3) = 18.$$

Finally,

$$3\left(\frac{P_{26} + \sqrt{3}P_{25}}{8P_{24}}\right) - 6\left(\frac{Q_{26} - Q_{24}}{Q_{25}}\right) = 6 + 18$$

$$= 24.$$

q: If $60^{\mathrm{a}}=3$ and $60^{\mathrm{b}}=5$ then the value of $12^{\frac{1-\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}}{2(1-\mathrm{b})}}$ equals :
question: If $60^{\mathrm{a}}=3$ and $60^{\mathrm{b}}=5$ then the value of $12^{\frac{1-\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}}{2(1-\mathrm{b})}}$ equals :
solution:
Given $60^a = 3$ and $60^b = 5$.

Taking logarithm with base 60, we get $a = \log_{60} 3$ and $b = \log_{60} 5$.

Look at the exponent $\dfrac{1-a-b}{2(1-b)}$.

For the numerator:

$$1 - a - b = \log_{60} 60 - \log_{60} 3 - \log_{60} 5 = \log_{60}\frac{60}{3 \cdot 5} = \log_{60} 4$$

For the denominator:

$$1 - b = \log_{60} 60 - \log_{60} 5 = \log_{60} 12$$

So the exponent becomes

$$\frac{\log_{60} 4}{2\log_{60} 12} = \frac{\log_{60} 4}{\log_{60} 12^2} = \frac{\log_{60} 4}{\log_{60} 144}$$

This is the same as $\log_{144} 4$.

$\log_{144} 4 = \dfrac{\log 4}{\log 144} = \dfrac{\log 4}{2\log 12} = \dfrac{1}{2}\log_{12} 4$.

So,

$$12^{\frac{1}{2}\log_{12} 4} = \left(12^{\log_{12} 4}\right)^{1/2} = 4^{1/2} = 2$$

The value is $2$.

q: In a school, out of 400 students, 300 students opt for at least one subject out of Biology, Chemistry and Mathematics. If 120 students opted for Biology, 150 students opted for Chemistry, 200 students opted for Mathematics and 30 opted for all the three subjects, find the number of students who opted for exactly two subjects.
question: In a school, out of 400 students, 300 students opt for at least one subject out of Biology, Chemistry and Mathematics. If 120 students opted for Biology, 150 students opted for Chemistry, 200 students opted for Mathematics and 30 opted for all the three subjects, find the number of students who opted for exactly two subjects.
solution:
Let the sets of students opting for Biology, Chemistry and Mathematics be $B$, $C$ and $M$.

Given:

$$ n(B \cup C \cup M)=300 $$

$$ n(B)=120,\quad n(C)=150,\quad n(M)=200 $$

$$ n(B \cap C \cap M)=30 $$

Using the formula for three sets,

$$ n(B \cup C \cup M)=n(B)+n(C)+n(M)-n(B \cap C)-n(C \cap M)-n(M \cap B)+n(B \cap C \cap M) $$

Substitute the values:

$$ 300=120+150+200-\{n(B \cap C)+n(C \cap M)+n(M \cap B)\}+30 $$

$$ 300=500-\{n(B \cap C)+n(C \cap M)+n(M \cap B)\} $$

So,

$$ n(B \cap C)+n(C \cap M)+n(M \cap B)=200 $$

This $200$ includes students who opted for all three subjects.

Since the $30$ students who opted for all three are counted in each of the three pairwise intersections, they are counted $3$ times.

To find students who opted for exactly two subjects, subtract these three counts of the all-three students:

$$ 200-3 \times 30=200-90=110 $$

So, the number of students who opted for exactly two subjects is

$$ \boxed{110} $$
